critic_raw: Second pass. Lab normalization is described as "per-type using training set statistics." In a CHF cohort, lab ranges can be substantially different from general population norms. Patients may have chronically abnormal lab values (e.g., elevated creatinine due to cardiorenal syndrome) that represent their individual baseline rather than acute derangement. Per-type normalization (across all patients) may lose the clinical signal of deviation from individual baseline. Does this weaken the sequence model's ability to detect meaningful changes?
defender_raw: Per-type normalization across patients is the standard approach and is correctly fit on training data only. The temporal dynamics of the sequence (changes between consecutive lab values, trends over time) are encoded in the sequence itself - the model can learn to compare adjacent values and detect trajectories. The model is not limited to interpreting each lab value in isolation; the BiLSTM's recurrent structure specifically enables it to model sequences of values and their changes. Individual baseline deviation would require patient-specific normalization, which would introduce leakage if estimated from the full patient history across splits.
adjudication_raw: Run 2: No unresolved flaw. Verdict: defense_wins.
